name: npm Registry API
what_it_does: The npm Registry API provides programmatic access to the world's largest JavaScript package registry, enabling package discovery, metadata retrieval, version listing, download statistics, and publishing. Agents can query package information, resolve dependency trees, check for updates, and publish new packages.
use_cases: Resolving package metadata and version manifests for dependency analysis
Checking for known vulnerabilities or outdated packages in a project
Discovering packages by keyword or author for automated tooling
Fetching download counts and popularity metrics for package evaluation
Publishing new package versions or dist-tags as part of a CI/CD pipeline
not_for: Actual package installation (use npm CLI or package manager directly)
Private package management without npm Teams/Pro subscription
best_when: An agent needs to inspect, resolve, or publish JavaScript/Node.js packages as part of an automated build or analysis workflow.
avoid_when: You need to install packages into a filesystem (use npm CLI), or need private package support without an npm paid plan.

auth.notes: Read operations on public packages require no authentication. Publishing and private package access require an npm access token generated via `npm token create`. Tokens are scoped to read-only or automation use. No OAuth flow; token management is via npm CLI or the npmjs.com web UI.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Unlimited read access to all public packages; publishing public packages is free
pricing.paid_tiers: npm Pro ($7/month) for private packages; npm Teams ($3/user/month) for org-level private packages
pricing.notes: Public registry reads are fully free and unauthenticated. Private packages require a paid plan. The registry itself is rate-limited only at very high unauthenticated volumes.

performance.rate_limits: No hard published rate limits for authenticated reads; unauthenticated requests may be throttled at high volume. CDN-backed for package tarballs.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Error responses are basic JSON with a short error string field. Missing structured error codes or actionable guidance. 404s for unknown packages are clear, but rate limit or auth errors are sparse.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Read operations are fully idempotent. Package publishing is not idempotent (cannot re-publish the same version). dist-tag updates are idempotent.
known_agent_gotchas: Package names are case-sensitive but npm lowercases all names — mixed-case queries may 404 unexpectedly
The /-/all endpoint returns a massive JSON payload (100MB+) and should never be fetched by agents; use the search endpoint instead
Published versions cannot be unpublished after 72 hours — agents must not publish test or malformed versions
The `dist.tarball` URL in package metadata requires a separate download step; the API does not serve tarballs inline
Scoped packages (e.g. @org/pkg) must be URL-encoded in the path as %40org%2Fpkg
Download count data is available via a separate stats API (api.npmjs.org) not the registry itself

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. npm tokens support read-only and automation scopes but lack fine-grained resource-level permissions. 2FA enforcement is available for publish operations on npmjs.com but not enforced by API callers. Supply chain concerns are significant in the npm ecosystem (typosquatting, dependency confusion).

score_rationale: The npm Registry API is highly functional for read-heavy workflows (package lookups, metadata resolution) and is freely accessible without auth. However, it lacks an MCP server, has sparse error messages, no documented retry guidance, and limited rate limit transparency. Agents must handle scoped package encoding, avoid the /-/all firehose, and can never recover from accidental publishes. Good for read-only dependency analysis; requires care for publish workflows.
